coredata.dc:title: Effect of airborne ultrasonic technology on microbial inactivation and quality of dried food ingredients
coredata.dc:creator: Charoux, Clémentine M.G.
O'Donnell, Colm P.
Tiwari, Brijesh K.
coredata.dc:description: Conventional methods for decontamination of dried foods have various limitations including undesired effects on both food quality and nutritional parameters. Airborne acoustic technology is a potential technology for microbial inactivation which has limited impacts on food quality. The objective of this study was to investigate the potential of airborne acoustic technology for the decontamination of black pepper grains (Piper nigrum L.) and tapioca starch (Manihot esculenta) inoculated with Bacillus subtilis vegetative cells and spores. Black pepper and tapioca starch samples were subjected to airborne acoustic waves at 170 W for treatment times of 0 (control) to 120 min at room temperature. Significant reductions of 2.19 log CFU/g and 2.01 log CFU/g were achieved after 30 min of treatment for black pepper and tapioca starch respectively (p < 0.05). No significant reductions were observed in spore contaminated samples (p > 0.05). Total phenolic content of black pepper and rheological properties of tapioca starch were not significantly influenced by airborne acoustic treatment at treatment times required to achieve 2 log reductions (p > 0.05). This study demonstrated that airborne acoustic technology can be employed for microbial inactivation of dried food ingredients with minimal impacts on food quality.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Airborne acoustic technology
Microbial inactivation
Scanning electron microscopy
Food quality
Dried ingredients

Phenolic content of black pepper grains

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental setup employed for airborne acoustic treatment for (a) non-contact type, (b) contact type and (c) focus area, size and intensity distribution (dB).
Sound pressure (dB) measurement at a frequency range from 0 to 40 kHz for indirect and direct contact set up.
Inactivation profile of vegetative cells of Bacillus subtilis inoculated black pepper (a) and tapioca starch (b) subjected to direct (●) and indirect (○) treatment using airborne acoustics.
Scanning Electron Microscopic images of Bacillus subtilis vegetative cells for non-treated (a) and acoustic treated (b).
Degradation of total phenolics for black pepper subjected to airborne acoustic treatment. a,b,cBar followed by different alphabets are significantly different (p < 0.05).
RVA profile of tapioca starch subjected to airborne acoustics.
Inactivation model parameters for vegetative cells of Bacillus subtilis inoculated black pepper and tapioca starch subjected to direct and indirect airborne acoustic treatment.

head.ce:title: Effect of airborne ultrasonic technology on microbial inactivation and quality of dried food ingredients

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Food Chemistry and Technology, Teagasc Food Research Centre, Dublin, Ireland

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biosystems and Food Engineering, University College Dublin, Dublin, Ireland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biosystems and Food Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Teagasc Food Research Centre, Ashtown, Dublin, Ireland.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Conventional methods for decontamination of dried foods have various limitations including undesired effects on both food quality and nutritional parameters. Airborne acoustic technology is a potential technology for microbial inactivation which has limited impacts on food quality. The objective of this study was to investigate the potential of airborne acoustic technology for the decontamination of black pepper grains (Piper nigrum L.) and tapioca starch (Manihot esculenta) inoculated with Bacillus subtilis vegetative cells and spores. Black pepper and tapioca starch samples were subjected to airborne acoustic waves at 170 W for treatment times of 0 (control) to 120 min at room temperature. Significant reductions of 2.19 log CFU/g and 2.01 log CFU/g were achieved after 30 min of treatment for black pepper and tapioca starch respectively (p < 0.05). No significant reductions were observed in spore contaminated samples (p > 0.05). Total phenolic content of black pepper and rheological properties of tapioca starch were not significantly influenced by airborne acoustic treatment at treatment times required to achieve 2 log reductions (p > 0.05). This study demonstrated that airborne acoustic technology can be employed for microbial inactivation of dried food ingredients with minimal impacts on food quality.

ce:section.ce:para: Dried food ingredients including grains, flours, herbs, spices and starches are widely used in cooking and commercial ready-to-eat products globally. Dried ingredients are often contaminated with heavy loads of vegetative bacteria and spore bacteria, and sometimes food pathogens, many of which can produce difficult-to-control biofilms on food processing surfaces [22]. Traditional thermal treatments are not an effective decontamination strategy for dried food ingredients as they often cause quality issues due to functionality and sensory impacts. Black pepper (Piper nigrum L.) is widely used in western and eastern cuisine due to its taste and flavour effects, and also for its preservative and medicinal properties [6]. Due to the presence of various bioactive molecules such as alkaloids (piperine), terpenes, and flavones, black pepper is known to exhibit antibacterial and antifungal effects, as well as antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antihypertensive, anticancer activities [1,3]. Similarly, native tapioca or cassava starch (Manihot esculenta) is used in a range of food formulations. Tapioca starch can form a clear and cohesive paste with a bland flavour, and is used in many food products including bread, cake, extruded cereals, sauces and beverages as a thickening and gelling agent [18]. These dried food ingredients are frequently contaminated with spoilage and/or pathogens microorganisms [23].

Conventional methods of decontamination of dried food ingredients are super-heated steam, ethylene oxide gas (EOG), ultraviolet irradiation and ionizing radiation. However these technologies have a number of limitations including the loss of sensory properties and high moisture content which favours mould development following super-heated steam treatment [17], potential carcinogenic effects from the residues of EOG fumigation [7] and the oxidation and degradation of components such as lipids after gamma-rays, x-rays or electron beams treatments [24]. Commercial chemical treatments such as fumigation using ethylene oxide are prohibited by EU directive 1991 due to the production of carcinogenic by-products [17].

Low frequency ultrasound employs high intensity waves which induce physical and/or chemical effects on food properties, and has strong potential for a range of food industry applications [12]. The use of ultrasound for decontamination has been widely demonstrated on liquid foods and foods immersed in liquid media using contact type systems (i.e. ultrasound bath and probe-based system) [14,20]. Non-contact ultrasonic applications have been investigated in the food industry for defoaming [15], drying [9] and inactivation of microorganisms [13]. Airborne acoustic ultrasound utilises non-contact transducers, which are capable of transmitting ultrasonic waves to a product using air as the coupling medium. Electrical energy is converted into mechanical vibrations in a piezoeletric transducer, which emits the ultrasonic waves through air towards the sample [4]. The objective of this study was to investigate the effects of airborne acoustic technology on (i) vegetative cells and bacterial spores, and (ii) key quality parameters of both black pepper grains and tapioca starch.

Black pepper (Piper nigrum L.) was purchased locally (Brand Heera, P&B Ltd, West Yorkshire, United Kingdom) and tapioca (Manihot esculenta) starch was obtained from AllinAll Ingredients Ltd (Product No 7111, Dublin, Ireland).

Bacillus subtilis DSM 618 (Merck KGaA, Germany) was grown in nutrient broth for 24 h at 30 °C under shaking conditions (150 RPM). B subtilis culture at 107 CFU/mL was inoculated into the selected dried food at a ratio 2:1 (v:w) and submitted to agitation (150 rpm) at 30 °C for 2 h. For contamination of samples by spores, Bacillus subtilis spore suspension (Merck KGaA, Germany, Product number 1.10649.0001) at 105 CFU/mL was heated at 80 °C for 10 min to inactivate potential vegetative cells. The dried ingredients were then inoculated with the spore suspension following the same protocol as the vegetative cells contaminated samples. After inoculation, the samples were dried at 35 °C for 90 min. The treatment was conducted as described in Section 2.3. One gram of sample was recuperated after 0 (the control was not submitted to sonication), 30, 60 and 90 min for vegetative cells, and 0, 60 and 120 min for spores, and resuspended into maximum recovery diluent (peptone saline broth) in order to obtain 1-fold dilution. Decimal dilutions were carried out and spread on nutrient agar plates. After 24 h at 30 °C, the CFU was counted. All experiments were replicated three times.

All experiments were carried out using an airborne ultrasonic system (Pusonics S.L., Madrid, Spain) operating at a frequency of 26 kHz with a maximum power output of 170 W. The system consists of an electronic power generator with a dynamic resonance controller, a power amplifier, a high impedance matching box, and a circular stepped-plate transducer. This type of transducer produces a highly focused acoustic field [8]. A hood was designed and customer built for the airborne system in order to avoid spreading of the powders and to intensify the acoustic field surrounding the sample (Fig. 1 a). The sample was placed at 42.5 cm from the focus centre of the transducer, at the maximum acoustic energy density of 10 W/cm2 (Fig. 1c). In the case of black pepper grains, two different experiments were carried out: non-contact application (the samples were placed at 42.5 cm from the plate) and direct contact application (the transducer was put upside down, the samples were placed on the stepped-plate, and a metal tray was placed 5 cm from the samples, acting as a reflector and generating standing waves) (Fig. 1b). The acoustic pressure inside the hood and on the top of the emitter was measured using a microphone (Model 40BE 1/4″ Pre polarized Free-Field, GRAS, UK).

The total phenolic content (TPC) of black peppers was determined using the Folin–Ciocalteu reagent (FCR) assay according to the method of Singleton et al. [19] with slight modifications as described by Rössle et al. [16]. The concentration of TPC was expressed in mg gallic acid equivalent (GAE) per g of dry weight of sample.

The pasting profile of tapioca starch was determined using the Rapid Visco Analyzer (RVA) (Model RVA 3D+, Newport Scientific, Australia). The tapioca starch suspension (2.5 g of starch mixed in 25 g of distilled water on 14% moisture basis) was heated from 50 to 95 °C at 6 °C/min (after equilibration for 1 min at 50 °C), held at 95 °C for 5 min, and cooled at 6 °C/min to 50 °C. Various pasting parameters including peak viscosity (PV), breakdown viscosity (BD), final viscosity (FV), setback viscosity (SB) and pasting temperature (PT) were determined from the pasting profile.

Analysis of variance (ANOVA) and separation of means was carried out using Minitab. Treatment means were separated using Tukeys’ test and considered significantly different at p < 0.05. All measurements were carried out in triplicate and average values reported.

Microbial inactivation curves obtained were fitted to Weibull model (Eq. (1)) distributions to determine microbial inactivation parameters using Gina FIT tool [10]. D values (Dv) were calculated using Eq. (2).
(1)
Log 10
(
N t
)
=
log 10
(
N 0
)
-
t δ
β
(2)
D V
= δ ×
x
1 / β
where Nt is the number of vegetative cells at treatment time (t, min) and N 0 is the initial number of cells, δ (min−1) is the time required to achieve 90% reduction in microbial load whereas, β is the shape factor, which corresponds to the shape of the microbial inactivation curve. The performance of the model was evaluated by calculating the coefficient of determination or R-squared noted R2 and the root mean square error noted RMSE. R2 was used to assess the goodness of fit of the regression model to the data, and RMSE was used to determine the absolute fit of the model to the data.

The sound pressure emitted by the airborne acoustic system was measured using a microphone (Fig. 2 ). The maximum acoustic sound pressure at the focus point for both the direct and indirect contact studies were 72.60 and 50.90 dB respectively. The peak of both curves was observed at 26 kHz, which corresponds to the frequency of the ultrasonic device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inactivation of Bacillus subtilis vegetative cells and spores
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 shows the microbial inactivation curve for black pepper and starch inoculated with vegetative cells of Bacillus subtilis. Significant reductions of vegetative cells in black pepper were observed from 7.56 ± 0.21 to 5.55 ± 0.39 log CFU/g after 30 min of indirect contact treatment and to 4.81 ± 0.20 log CFU/g after 90 min treatment (p < 0.05) (Fig. 3a). Whereas in the case of direct treatment of black pepper, significant reductions from 7.24 ± 0.14 to 5.84 ± 0.33 log CFU/g after 30 min of indirect contact treatment and to 4.72 ± 0.78 log CFU/g after 90 min treatment were observed (p < 0.05). In the case of tapioca starch significant reductions from 6.29 ± 0.24 to 4.22 ± 1.10 log CFU/g after 30 min and to 3.63 ± 0.66 log CFU/g after 90 min were observed (p < 0.05) (Fig. 3b). Direct contact studies for tapioca starch were not performed because the dried powder was blown away due to the airborne acoustics sound waves.

Weibull model parameters i.e. the shape factors (β), and the time to achieve 90% reduction (D value) in vegetative cells with corresponding RMSE and R2 values for direct and indirect treatments are shown in Table 1 . D values of 5.44 ± 3.28 min for indirect treatment and 14.00 ± 1.79 min for direct treatment were observed in the case of black pepper, while a D value of 2.67 ± 1.68 min for indirect treatment of starch samples was observed. The shape factor (β) value was found to be <1 indicating that the survival curves of the vegetative cells fitted to Weibull model showed a concave upward trend. A shape concave upward (β < 1) indicates that the rate of microbial inactivation decreases over time or concave downward (β > 1) if the rate of microbial inactivation increases overtime [5]. The Weibull model showed a good fit with low RMSE (<0.32) and high R2 (>0.98) values. Table 2 shows the effects of Bacillus subtilis spores inoculated black pepper and tapioca starch subjected to direct and indirect airborne acoustic treatment. Compared to vegetative cells Bacillus subtilis spores showed low reduction rates, reductions of 0.37 ± 0.30 and 0.15 ± 0.25 log after 60 min of indirect treatment for black pepper and tapioca starch were observed. Extended treatment time of additional 60 min did not show any significant increase in log reductions (p > 0.05).

Scanning electron microscopic images of the vegetative cells (Fig. 4 ) showed rupturing of cells. Fig. 4 reveals that Bacillus cells were fragmented and the physiological nature of Bacillus cells was altered. Similar fragmentation of bacterial cells is reported for high pressure treatments [25]. Fragmentation of cells due to high pressure causes significant microbial inactivation. Hence, from images it can be inferred that the microbial inactivation caused is mainly due to sound pressure effects. However, the effect of airborne acoustic was not observed in the case of Bacillus spores, whereas a study carried out by Hoover et al. [11] showed that 60 s of ultrasound treatment resulted in 98.12% and 99.99% inactivation of Bacillus thuringiensis spores at 161 kHz and 93 kHz respectively. Reported inactivation of vegetative cells subjected to ultrasound treatment in liquid medium is mainly due to inertial and transient cavitation. Microbial inactivation is mainly due to the formation, growth and collapse of cavitating bubbles causing sonoporation i.e. the formation of pores on cellular membrane owing to acoustic cavitation, micro-mechanical shockwaves, compression & rarefaction or sonochemical reactions [2,21]. However, in the case of airborne acoustics, cavitation is not responsible for microbial inactivation. The inactivation observed is mainly due to sound pressure, standing waves and micro-mechanical shockwaves. The mechanical energy released through these mechanisms can alter the morphology of microorganisms, inducing changing in their state and generating biochemical reactions which may cause the observed inactivation.

A significant change in moisture content was observed for both black pepper and starch samples. These changes in moisture content were considered when determining phenolic degradation and analysing starch profiles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phenolic content of black pepper grains
ce:section.ce:para: The phenolic content of untreated black pepper grains was 5.45 ± 1.09 mg GAE/g. The effects of direct and indirect treatments on phenolic content of black pepper are shown in Fig. 5 . A significant difference in the phenolic content of non-treated and ultrasound treated black pepper was found for indirect contact treatment longer than 30 min (p < 0.05). A significant reduction of up to 21.5% in phenolic content of black pepper grains was observed after 90 min of indirect contact treatment compared to control samples (p < 0.05). However, in the case of direct treatment no significant decrease was observed (p > 0.05). Considering that treatment times exceeding 30 min did not show higher log reductions, it is not necessary to consider the quality impact of airborne ultrasound for longer duration treatment time.

The pasting profile is a key quality parameter of starch. Pasting profiles of untreated and treated tapioca starch samples are shown in Fig. 6 and various rheological parameters of tapioca starch samples are summarised in Table 3 . Acoustic treatment did not cause any significant effects on peak, trough, breakdown, final viscosity, setback, peak time and pasting temperature of tapioca starch samples irrespective of treatment times (p > 0.05). This shows that airborne acoustic technology did not have a significant impact on the rheological properties of starch.

This study demonstrated that non-contact ultrasound inactivates vegetative cells by 2.01 log CFU/g and 2.07 log CFU/g after 30 min of indirect treatment in black pepper and tapioca starch respectively (p < 0.05). Key quality parameters namely total phenolic content of black pepper and the rheological profiles of tapioca starch were not significantly affected by airborne acoustic ultrasound treatment at a treatment time of 30 min (p > 0.05). However, even though airborne acoustic technology has strong potential for microbial inactivation of vegetative cells, the energy released is not sufficient to inactivate bacterial spores (p > 0.05).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was carried out with the financial support of the Irish Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine.
